source_metadata.title_raw: Stephen M. Fischer - A-19-122 - January 14, 2020 - Oxnard

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886
Stephen M. Fischer 
Office of the City Attorney 
305 West Third Street, Suite lOOE 
Oxnard, CA 93030 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-122 
Dear Mr. Fischer: 
January 14, 2020 
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Alexander Nguyen, City 
Manager (Mr. Nguyen or the City Manager) for the City of Oxnard (the City) regarding the conflict 
of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the Act) 1 and Section 1090. Please note that we 
are not a finder of fact when rendering advice and any advice we provide assumes your facts are 
complete and accurate. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71) 
Under Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating 
to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Ventura County District Attorney's Office, 
which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either 
entity. (Section 1097.1 ( c )( 4 ). ) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 
1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other 
than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1 ( c )( 5).) 
QUESTIONS 
, 1. 
Does the Act or Section 1090 prohibit Mr. Nguyen from participating in the City's
decisions regarding an exclusive negotiating agreement for potential development of properties in 
the City's downtown area (the project) with Madison Park Financial Corp. (MPF) for 12 months 
following the City Manager's divestment from his previous investments in MPF projects in other 
jurisdictions? 
2.
If Mr. Nguyen does not participate in the decisions, would Government Code
Section 1090 prohibit the City from entering into an exclusive agreement and other agreements with 
MPF regarding the project 12 months after Mr. Nguyen divests his interests in MPF? 2
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 ofTitle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Your request originally asked whether Mr. Nguyen was required to report the sale of his investments, which 
had not yet occurred, as a source of income because the buyer's identity would be unknown. You provided updated 
facts stating that the investments were sold, and the buyer's identity is known. Therefore, this question is moot. 


CONCLUSION 
File No. A-19-122 
Page No. 2 
1. 
Yes. Under the Act, if no other economic interests exist, then Mr. Nguyen is 
prohibited from making, participating in making, or influencing a City decision regarding MPF 
before, but not after the 12-month period commencing on May 21, 2019. Under Section 1090, Mr. 
Nguyen is required to divest his interests before participating in the making of a contract between 
the City and MPF, which he has done. 
2. 
No. Because Mr. Nguyen has completely divested his investment interests, Section 
1090 does not prohibit the City from entering into a contract with MPF. 
FACTS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTER 
A. 
Divestment of the City Manager's Interests. 
The City and the Parking Authority of the City of Oxnard own properties in downtown 
Oxnard that the City seeks to develop. MPF has expressed an interest in negotiating an "exclusive 
negotiating agreement" with the City for development of the Properties. 
In 2010, 2014 and 2016, the City Manager invested in three real estate projects located in 
Northern California, outside the City's jurisdiction (the investments). The investments were made 
with the following LLC entities, each of which was managed by MPF: (1) Madison Park Investors 
2, LLC; (2) Madison Park Investors 3, LLC; and (3) 3900 Adeline Partners, LLC (collectively the 
LLC purchasers). The City Manager invested in the initial share offerings for each of the LLC 
purchasers. The investments, which were not publicly traded, totaled $200,000. The City Manager 
received regular distributions and returns of capital on the investments. The last regular distribution 
and return, which exceeded $500, was paid to the City Manager on May 10, 2019. 
On May 21, 2019, the City Manager decided to divest all his ownership shares in the 
investments. Since then, he has received no regular distributions or returns of capital, and the only 
proceeds he received were from his sale of the investments. MPF facilitated the sale. When a 
member of an investment group elects to liquidate shares, MPF either: ( 1) facilitates a sale between 
the parties involved; or (2) if appropriate, allow the LLC entities to buy back the units with cash on 
hand. No other members expressed interest in acquiring more units, but the LLC purchasers had 
cash on hand. In its capacity as manager and fiduciary for the LLC purchasers, MPF effected the 
purchase of the City Manager's shares by the LLC purchasers as part of its duties after no other 
parties had expressed an interest to acquire more shares. 
The City Manager's ownership interests in the LLC purchasers were as follows: (1) 0.37% 
in Madison Park Investors 3, LLC; (2) 1.18% in 3900 Adeline Partners, LLC; and (3) 0.80% in 
Madison Park Investors 2, LLC. He did not have a controlling interest in the LLC purchasers. He 
was a regular investor member with 40-60 other investor members, and he owned less than 5% of 
each LLC purchaser's shares. He owned voting shares proportional to his ownership interests. 
There was no shareholder vote on the purchase of the City Manager's investments. 


B. 
Ownership of the City Manager's Interests. 
File No. A-19-122 
Page No. 3 
MPF's owners are JPRG Family Enterprises, LLC, which owns 99.27%, and Simon Chen, 
MPF's Chief Operating Officer, who owns 0.73%. 
JPRG Family Enterprises, LLC is owned 50% by MPF's founder John Protopappas, and 
50% by his ex-wife Randi Gallenson under their respective trusts. 
The LLC purchasers are owned by the collective members of each LLC. Each of the LLC 
purchasers has approximately 40-60 members, each of whom has somewhere between a 0.01 % and 
10.0% ownership interest. Collectively, the LLC members own 100% of the LLC purchasers. MPF 
acts as manager for each of the LLC purchasers, along with the other investment funds it sponsors. 
Consequently, the President & CEO ofMPF controls both MPF and each LLC purchaser. 
MPF's ownership interests in the LLC purchasers are: (1) Madison Park Investors 3, LLC-
0.70%; (2) 3900 Adeline Partners, LLC - 2.9%; and (3) Madison Park Investors 2, LLC - none. 
No person or group has 50% or more ownership in MPF and the LLC purchasers. John 
Protopappas, President & CEO of MPF, owns less than 50.0% interest in MPF and he owns, 
directly or indirectly, no more than 3.7% of any of the LLC purchasers. Mr. Protopappas and his ex-
wife together own 99.27% ofMPF, and no more than 7.49% of any of the LLC purchasers. JPRG 
Family Enterprises, LLC has a 7.49% interest in Madison Park Investors 2, LLC. 
ANALYSIS 
A. 
The Act. 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning 
of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on 
one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) 
As City Manager, Mr. Nguyen is a "public official" subject to the Act's conflict of interest 
provisions. (Sections 87100, 87103, 87200; Regulation 18700(b)(l).) He will also be making, 
participating in making, or influencing governmental decisions under the Act when taking part in 
the City's decisions regarding a contract with MPF. (See Regulations 18702.1-18702.3.) 
Section 87103 sets forth the interests from which a conflict of interest may arise under the 
Act.3 The circumstances here involve the following economic interests: 
• 
Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).) 
3 Mr. Nguyen's personal finances, including his immediate family, are also potentially disqualifying. (Section 
87103.) 


File No. A-19-122 
Page No. 4 
• 
Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two 
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. (Section 87103(b).) 
• 
Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
According to the facts, Mr. Nguyen has received income from the LCC purchasers including 
his last distribution from the investments on May 10, 2019, and the proceeds from the sale of his 
ownership shares in the investments on May 21, 2019. However, for purpose of this analysis, the 
pertinent question is whether Mr. Nguyen has an interest in MPF. Under Regulation 18700.2(c) an 
official with an interest in a business entity, including a business entity that is a source of income, 
also has an interest in a parent, subsidiary, or any other related business of the source of income. 
Business entities, other than a parent corporation, are "otherwise related" if: "(A) The same 
person or persons together direct or control each business entity; or (B) The same person or persons 
together have a 50 percent or greater ownership interest in each business entity." (Regulation 
18700.2(b)(3).) In this case, MPF and each of the LLC purchasers are "otherwise related business 
entities" because the President & CEO of MPF manages and controls both MPF and each LLC 
purchaser. Thus, Mr. Nguyen has a financial interest in MPF and each LLC purchaser. 
1. Foreseeability and Materiality. 
Foreseeability standards vary depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in a 
governmental decision. An official's financial interest is explicitly involved in a decision, and is 
presumed to be reasonably foreseeable, if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a 
decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) Regulation 18701(a) 
establishes that a financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the 
issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or 
contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision affecting a real 
property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l)-(6). 
For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision at issue, the financial 
effect of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701 (b ). ) 
The reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on an official's 
financial interest in a source of income is also material if the source is a named party in, or the 
subject of, the decision including a claimant, applicant, respondent, or contracting party. 
(Regulation 18702.3(a)(l).) If the business entity is not explicitly involved in a City decision, then 
the financial effect is material if "[t]he decision may result in an increase or decrease of the entity's 
annual gross revenues, or the value of the entity's assets or liabilities" as specified in Regulation 
18702.l(a)(2), or "[t]he decision may cause the entity to incur or avoid additional expenses or to 


File No. A-19-122 
Page No. 5 
reduce or eliminate expenses in an amount equal to or more than" the amounts set forth under 
Regulation 18702.l(a)(3). (Regulation 18702.3(A)(4).) 
2. The City's Decisions Regarding MPF or Any of the LLC Purchasers. 
Where MPF or any of the LLC purchasers are explicitly involved in a City decision, the 
financial effect is reasonably foreseeable (Regulation 1870l(a)), and the financial effect will be 
presumed material under Regulation 18702.1 ( a)(l) applies. 
Regulation 18704(a) provides: "A public official makes a governmental decision if the 
official authorizes or directs any action, votes, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her 
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her 
agency." Regulation 18704(b) states: "A public official participates in a governmental decision if 
the official provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation for the purpose of affecting the 
decision without significant intervening substantive review." 
During the 12-month period commencing on May 21, 2019, Mr. Nguy en is disqualified 
from any decision in which MPF or any of the LLC purchasers is explicitly involved in the 
decision.4 Thus, for the 12-month period, he may not participate as City Manager in negotiations 
between the City and MPF for an exclusive negotiating agreement. If the City engages in 
negotiations with MPF during the 12-month period, then Mr. Nguyen must recuse himself pursuant 
to Section 87105 and Regulation 18707. 
B. 
Section 1090. 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. The prohibition applies regardless of 
whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 
Cal.3d 633, 646.) Section 1090 concerns financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, 
that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering 
the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) "[A]n official has a 
financial interest in a contract if he might profit from it." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 
289, 333.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the 
appearance of impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
Under Section 1090, Mr. Nguyen is required to complete his divestment before participating 
in the making of a contract between the City and MPF. (See Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 187 (2003) (finding 
there would be no financial interest under Section 1090 "if the board member first terminates his 
business relationship with the firm").) Because Mr. Nguyen has divested his financial interests, 
Section 1090 does not prohibit him from participating as a City official in the making of, or the City 
from entering into, a contract with MPF. 
4 Depending on the circumstances, Mr. Nguyen may be disqualified where one of these entities is not explicitly 
involved in the decision. The detennination of a conflict of interest is dependent upon the facts involved in each 
decision. Therefore, we recommend you contact our office for additional advice regarding Mr. Nguyen's participation 
in any other City decisions that may involve MPF or any of the LLC purchasers. 


File No. A-19-122 
Page No. 6 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
JMF:sal 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge

. Feser Jr. 
enior Counsel, Legal Division

sections.analysis:
A. 
The Act. 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning 
of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on 
one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) 
As City Manager, Mr. Nguyen is a "public official" subject to the Act's conflict of interest 
provisions. (Sections 87100, 87103, 87200; Regulation 18700(b)(l).) He will also be making, 
participating in making, or influencing governmental decisions under the Act when taking part in 
the City's decisions regarding a contract with MPF. (See Regulations 18702.1-18702.3.) 
Section 87103 sets forth the interests from which a conflict of interest may arise under the 
Act.3 The circumstances here involve the following economic interests: 
• 
Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).) 
3 Mr. Nguyen's personal finances, including his immediate family, are also potentially disqualifying. (Section 
87103.)  
• 
Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two 
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. (Section 87103(b).) 
• 
Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
According to the facts, Mr. Nguyen has received income from the LCC purchasers including 
his last distribution from the investments on May 10, 2019, and the proceeds from the sale of his 
ownership shares in the investments on May 21, 2019. However, for purpose of this analysis, the 
pertinent question is whether Mr. Nguyen has an interest in MPF. Under Regulation 18700.2(c) an 
official with an interest in a business entity, including a business entity that is a source of income, 
also has an interest in a parent, subsidiary, or any other related business of the source of income. 
Business entities, other than a parent corporation, are "otherwise related" if: "(A) The same 
person or persons together direct or control each business entity; or (B) The same person or persons 
together have a 50 percent or greater ownership interest in each business entity." (Regulation 
18700.2(b)(3).) In this case, MPF and each of the LLC purchasers are "otherwise related business 
entities" because the President & CEO of MPF manages and controls both MPF and each LLC 
purchaser. Thus, Mr. Nguyen has a financial interest in MPF and each LLC purchaser. 
1. Foreseeability and Materiality. 
Foreseeability standards vary depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in a 
governmental decision. An official's financial interest is explicitly involved in a decision, and is 
presumed to be reasonably foreseeable, if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a 
decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) Regulation 18701(a) 
establishes that a financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the 
issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or 
contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision affecting a real 
property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l)-(6). 
For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision at issue, the financial 
effect of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701 (b ). ) 
The reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on an official's 
financial interest in a source of income is also material if the source is a named party in, or the 
subject of, the decision including a claimant, applicant, respondent, or contracting party. 
(Regulation 18702.3(a)(l).) If the business entity is not explicitly involved in a City decision, then 
the financial effect is material if "[t]he decision may result in an increase or decrease of the entity's 
annual gross revenues, or the value of the entity's assets or liabilities" as specified in Regulation 
18702.l(a)(2), or "[t]he decision may cause the entity to incur or avoid additional expenses or to  
reduce or eliminate expenses in an amount equal to or more than" the amounts set forth under 
Regulation 18702.l(a)(3). (Regulation 18702.3(A)(4).) 
2. The City's Decisions Regarding MPF or Any of the LLC Purchasers. 
Where MPF or any of the LLC purchasers are explicitly involved in a City decision, the 
financial effect is reasonably foreseeable (Regulation 1870l(a)), and the financial effect will be 
presumed material under Regulation 18702.1 ( a)(l) applies. 
Regulation 18704(a) provides: "A public official makes a governmental decision if the 
official authorizes or directs any action, votes, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her 
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her 
agency." Regulation 18704(b) states: "A public official participates in a governmental decision if 
the official provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation for the purpose of affecting the 
decision without significant intervening substantive review." 
During the 12-month period commencing on May 21, 2019, Mr. Nguy en is disqualified 
from any decision in which MPF or any of the LLC purchasers is explicitly involved in the 
decision.4 Thus, for the 12-month period, he may not participate as City Manager in negotiations 
between the City and MPF for an exclusive negotiating agreement. If the City engages in 
negotiations with MPF during the 12-month period, then Mr. Nguyen must recuse himself pursuant 
to Section 87105 and Regulation 18707. 
B. 
Section 1090. 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. The prohibition applies regardless of

financial interest in a contract if he might profit from it." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 
289, 333.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the

business relationship with the firm").) Because Mr. Nguyen has divested his financial interests,

from entering into, a contract with MPF. 
4 Depending on the circumstances, Mr. Nguyen may be disqualified where one of these entities is not explicitly 
involved in the decision. The detennination of a conflict of interest is dependent upon the facts involved in each 
decision. Therefore, we recommend you contact our office for additional advice regarding Mr. Nguyen's participation 
in any other City decisions that may involve MPF or any of the LLC purchasers.

parties had expressed an interest to acquire more shares. 
The City Manager's ownership interests in the LLC purchasers were as follows: (1) 0.37% 
in Madison Park Investors 3, LLC; (2) 1.18% in 3900 Adeline Partners, LLC; and (3) 0.80% in 
Madison Park Investors 2, LLC. He did not have a controlling interest in the LLC purchasers. He 
was a regular investor member with 40-60 other investor members, and he owned less than 5% of 
each LLC purchaser's shares. He owned voting shares proportional to his ownership interests. 
There was no shareholder vote on the purchase of the City Manager's investments. 


B. 
Ownership of the City Manager's Interests.  
MPF's owners are JPRG Family Enterprises, LLC, which owns 99.27%, and Simon Chen, 
MPF's Chief Operating Officer, who owns 0.73%. 
JPRG Family Enterprises, LLC is owned 50% by MPF's founder John Protopappas, and 
50% by his ex-wife Randi Gallenson under their respective trusts. 
The LLC purchasers are owned by the collective members of each LLC. Each of the LLC 
purchasers has approximately 40-60 members, each of whom has somewhere between a 0.01 % and 
10.0% ownership interest. Collectively, the LLC members own 100% of the LLC purchasers. MPF 
acts as manager for each of the LLC purchasers, along with the other investment funds it sponsors. 
Consequently, the President & CEO ofMPF controls both MPF and each LLC purchaser. 
MPF's ownership interests in the LLC purchasers are: (1) Madison Park Investors 3, LLC-
0.70%; (2) 3900 Adeline Partners, LLC - 2.9%; and (3) Madison Park Investors 2, LLC - none. 
No person or group has 50% or more ownership in MPF and the LLC purchasers. John 
Protopappas, President & CEO of MPF, owns less than 50.0% interest in MPF and he owns, 
directly or indirectly, no more than 3.7% of any of the LLC purchasers. Mr. Protopappas and his ex-
wife together own 99.27% ofMPF, and no more than 7.49% of any of the LLC purchasers. JPRG 
Family Enterprises, LLC has a 7.49% interest in Madison Park Investors 2, LLC.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Stephen M. Fischer Office of the City Attorney 305 West Third Street, Suite lOOE Oxnard, CA 93030 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-19-122 Dear Mr. Fischer: January 14, 2020 This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Alexander Nguyen, City Manager (Mr. Nguyen or the City Manager) for the City of Oxnard (the City) regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the Act) 1 and Section 1090. Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71) Under Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Ventura County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1 ( c )( 4 ). ) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1 ( c )( 5).) QUESTIONS , 1. Does the Act or Section 1090 prohibit Mr. Nguyen from participating in the City's decisions regarding an exclusive negotiating agreement for potential development of properties in the City's downtown area (the project) with Madison Park Financial Corp. (MPF) for 12 months following the City Manager's divestment from his previous investments in MPF projects in other jurisdictions? 2. If Mr. Nguyen does not participate in the decisions, would Government Code Section 1090 prohibit the City from entering into an exclusive agreement and other agreements with MPF regarding the project 12 months after Mr. Nguyen divests his interests in MPF? 2 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 ofTitle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 2 Your request originally asked whether Mr. Nguyen was required to report the sale of his investments, which had not yet occurred, as a source of income because the buyer's identity would be unknown. You provided updated facts stating that the investments were sold, and the buyer's identity is known. Therefore, this question is moot. CONCLUSION File No. A-19-122 Page No. 2 1. Yes. Under the Act, if no other economic interests exist, then Mr. Nguyen is prohibited from making, participating in making, or influencing a City decision regarding MPF before, but not after the 12-month period commencing on May 21, 2019. Under Section 1090, Mr.
